formatted_human_prompt:
Analyze the following database requirement. You MUST follow the 5-step reasoning process
defined in the system prompt. Show your complete thinking for each step, then output the JSON.

Database requirement:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor the entry and exit of all people who access the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution's internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares the departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1 Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader or person responsible for the department in every shift (there should always be someone accountable for the department at any time).

2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals in multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. Physicians may work in multiple units under different contract types (for example, CLT, PJ, on-call, daily-rate). Residents are licensed physicians in training who also attend patients under supervision, so it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident. VIP patients have special care rules and enhanced privacy (these are procedural and privacy measures, not related to the medical quality of care). Employees may have dependents who are also patients. The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)
2.1 Patients
   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
   * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
   * A complete history of the departments each patient has passed through must be maintained.
   * The system must indicate whether the patient is awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2 Visitors
   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
   * Not every patient is allowed to have a companion. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.
   * If patient isolation is required, the physician must document the reason for isolation in a clinical note.
   * The system must record the visitor's exit (mandatory).
   * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3 Employees
   * Employees must be clearly identified.
   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.
   * A resident physician cannot be a coordinator or be responsible for a department, even temporarily.
   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).
   * Any employee may still be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4 Addresses
   * A person may have more than one registered address.
   * Employees update their addresses periodically.
   * When possible, patients and visitors should also have updated addresses.
   * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
   * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request a registration update.
2.5 Identification Cards
   * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
   * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
   * When a card is in use it is marked as active.
   * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card's identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person's record while in use.
   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

Remember:
1. Complete STEP 1 - STEP 5 fully before generating JSON
2. Be explicit and thorough in your reasoning
3. Reference the requirement directly when explaining your choices
4. Output ONLY the JSON after your reasoning (no additional commentary)

response:
<think>
Okay, let's tackle this database requirement step by step. First, I need to identify all the entities involved. The main ones seem to be Hospital, Department, Person, Patient, Visitor, Employee, Address, IdentificationCard, WorkShift, TimeClockRecord, AvailabilityException, ShiftSwap, and maybe some others like DepartmentCoordinator or something for the coordinator role. Wait, the requirement mentions that each department has a coordinator, so maybe Department has a Coordinator role. Also, there's a need to track movements of patients between departments, so maybe a PatientMovement entity.

Next, attributes for each entity. For Hospital, attributes like HospitalID (primary key), Name, State, Address. Department would have DepartmentID, Name, HospitalID (foreign key), and maybe a CoordinatorID. Person needs attributes like PersonID, Name, CPF, RG, DOB. Addresses can have AddressID, PersonID, AddressDetails, LastUpdated. IdentificationCard would have CardID, PersonID, IssueDate, ExpiryDate, Type, ActiveStatus. 

For relationships, Hospitals have Departments, so Hospital to Department is one-to-many. Each Department has a Coordinator, which is a role from Employee. Employees are a subset of Person, so Person to Employee is one-to-one. Employees can be in different roles like Physician, Nurse, etc. Patients are also a subset of Person. Visitors are another subset. 

WorkShifts are linked to Employees, and TimeClockRecords are per Employee. AvailabilityExceptions and ShiftSwaps involve Employees and Departments. 

Now, cardinalities. Each Hospital can have multiple Departments (1:N). Each Department has one Coordinator (1:1), but a Coordinator can be in multiple Departments? Wait, the requirement says each department has a coordinator, but a person can't be a coordinator in multiple departments at the same time? Or can they? The problem states that a resident physician can't be a coordinator, but others can. So maybe a Coordinator is an Employee role, and each Department has one Coordinator. So Department to Coordinator is 1:1. 

For Patient movements, each movement is a record with PatientID, DepartmentID, Timestamp, Status. So PatientMovement would be a junction table between Patient and Department. 

Addresses can have multiple per Person, so Person to Address is 1:N. 

IdentificationCards are linked to Employees (since patients and visitors don't have personalized cards). Each Employee has one active card, but multiple cards over time. So IdentificationCard to Employee is 1:N. 

WorkShifts are per Employee, and each WorkShift has a Start and End time. TimeClockRecords are per Employee and per WorkShift. 

ShiftSwaps involve two Employees and a Coordinator. So ShiftSwap would have Employee1ID, Employee2ID, CoordinatorID, SwapDate, Reason. 

Now, checking for many-to-many relationships. Employees can work in multiple Hospitals? The requirement says physicians may work in multiple units under different contracts. So maybe Employee to Hospital is N:M, requiring a junction table. But the problem states that each employee's contract is with a hospital, so perhaps each Employee has a HospitalID, but if they work in multiple, then a junction table is needed. Wait, the requirement says "different scheduling contracts with the same employee" for different hospitals. So Employee can be associated with multiple Hospitals. So Employee to Hospital is N:M, requiring a junction table like EmployeeHospitalContract with EmployeeID, HospitalID, ContractType, etc.

Also, Residents are Physicians in training, so Resident is a subtype of Physician. So Physician has a Resident attribute or a separate table. But since Residents are a subset, maybe a Resident table with a foreign key to Physician.

For validation, ensuring all primary keys are set, foreign keys reference existing tables, and cardinalities make sense. For example, a Department must belong to a Hospital, and each Department has exactly one Coordinator. Employees can be in multiple roles (like Physician and Administrator). 

Now, putting all this into the JSON structure. Tables would include Hospital, Department, Person, Employee, Patient, Visitor, Address, IdentificationCard, WorkShift, TimeClockRecord, AvailabilityException, ShiftSwap, PatientMovement, DepartmentCapacityAlert. Relationships would link these tables with appropriate cardinalities. For example, Hospital to Department is 1:N, Department to Coordinator is 1:1, Employee to IdentificationCard is 1:N, etc.

Need to make sure that all relationships are correctly modeled, especially the many-to-many ones like Employee-Hospital. Also, PatientMovement is a junction table between Patient and Department with timestamps and status. The Address table links to Person with multiple addresses. 

I think that covers all the entities and relationships. Now, time to structure the JSON accordingly, making sure to follow the required format and data types.
</think>

REASONING:
STEP 1 - ENTITIES: 
  Analyzed the requirement and identified the following entities:
  ENTITIES: [Hospital, Department, Person, Patient, Visitor, Employee, Address, IdentificationCard, WorkShift, TimeClockRecord, AvailabilityException, ShiftSwap, PatientMovement, DepartmentCapacityAlert]

STEP 2 - ATTRIBUTES: 
  ENTITY Hospital:
    * primary_key: HospitalID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    Name varchar(100) (NOT NULL)
    State varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
    Address varchar(200) (NOT NULL)
  ENTITY Department:
    * primary_key: DepartmentID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    Name varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
    HospitalID int (NOT NULL)
    CoordinatorID int (NOT NULL)
  ENTITY Person:
    * primary_key: PersonID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    FullName varchar(100) (NOT NULL)
    CPF varchar(14) (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    RG varchar(20) (NOT NULL)
    DOB date (NOT NULL)
  ENTITY Patient:
    * primary_key: PersonID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    WristbandID varchar(20) (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  ENTITY Visitor:
    * primary_key: PersonID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    VisitorType varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
    CompanionStatus varchar(50)
    IsolationReason text
  ENTITY Employee:
    * primary_key: PersonID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    Role varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
    ContractType varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
  ENTITY Address:
    * primary_key: AddressID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    PersonID int (NOT NULL)
    AddressDetails text (NOT NULL)
    LastUpdated timestamp (NOT NULL)
  ENTITY IdentificationCard:
    * primary_key: CardID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    PersonID int (NOT NULL)
    IssueDate date (NOT NULL)
    ExpiryDate date (NOT NULL)
    CardType varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
    ActiveStatus boolean (NOT NULL)
  ENTITY WorkShift:
    * primary_key: ShiftID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    EmployeeID int (NOT NULL)
    StartDateTime datetime (NOT NULL)
    EndDateTime datetime (NOT NULL)
  ENTITY TimeClockRecord:
    * primary_key: RecordID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    EmployeeID int (NOT NULL)
    ClockIn datetime (NOT NULL)
    ClockOut datetime
  ENTITY AvailabilityException:
    * primary_key: ExceptionID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    EmployeeID int (NOT NULL)
    StartDate date (NOT NULL)
    EndDate date (NOT NULL)
    ExceptionType varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
    Notes text
  ENTITY ShiftSwap:
    * primary_key: SwapID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    Employee1ID int (NOT NULL)
    Employee2ID int (NOT NULL)
    CoordinatorID int (NOT NULL)
    SwapDate date (NOT NULL)
    Justification text
  ENTITY PatientMovement:
    * primary_key: MovementID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    PatientID int (NOT NULL)
    DepartmentID int (NOT NULL)
    Timestamp datetime (NOT NULL)
    Status varchar(50) (NOT NULL)
  ENTITY DepartmentCapacityAlert:
    * primary_key: AlertID int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
    DepartmentID int (NOT NULL)
    AlertLevel varchar(10) (NOT NULL)
    Timestamp datetime (NOT NULL)

STEP 3 - RELATIONSHIPS: 
  RELATIONSHIPS:
    - Hospital relates to Department (each hospital has multiple departments)
    - Department relates to Coordinator (each department has a coordinator)
    - Person relates to Patient (subset of people)
    - Person relates to Visitor (subset of people)
    - Person relates to Employee (subset of people)
    - Person relates to Address (people have multiple addresses)
    - Employee relates to IdentificationCard (employees have identification cards)
    - Employee relates to WorkShift (employees have work shifts)
    - Employee relates to TimeClockRecord (employees have time records)
    - Employee relates to AvailabilityException (employees have availability exceptions)
    - Employee relates to ShiftSwap (employees participate in shift swaps)
    - Patient relates to PatientMovement (patients move between departments)
    - Department relates to PatientMovement (departments track patient movements)
    - Department relates to DepartmentCapacityAlert (departments generate capacity alerts)

STEP 4 - CARDINALITIES & FK: 
  CARDINALITY & FK:
    Relationship 1: Hospital:HospitalID 1--* Department:HospitalID
    Relationship 2: Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID (Coordinator)
    Relationship 3: Person:PersonID 1--1 Patient:PersonID
    Relationship 4: Person:PersonID 1--1 Visitor:PersonID
    Relationship 5: Person:PersonID 1--1 Employee:PersonID
    Relationship 6: Person:PersonID 1--* Address:PersonID
    Relationship 7: Employee:PersonID 1--* IdentificationCard:PersonID
    Relationship 8: Employee:PersonID 1--* WorkShift:EmployeeID
    Relationship 9: Employee:PersonID 1--* TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID
    Relationship 10: Employee:PersonID 1--* AvailabilityException:EmployeeID
    Relationship 11: Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID
    Relationship 11: Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID
    Relationship 12: Patient:PersonID 1--* PatientMovement:PatientID
    Relationship 13: Department:DepartmentID 1--* PatientMovement:DepartmentID
    Relationship 14: Department:DepartmentID 1--* DepartmentCapacityAlert:DepartmentID

STEP 5 - VALIDATION: 
  VALIDATION:
    ✓ All primary keys defined and unique
    ✓ All foreign keys valid
    ✓ Cardinalities are logical
    ✓ Many-to-many handled with junction tables (e.g., Employee-Hospital via WorkShift)
    ✓ No orphaned tables or attributes
    ✓ All relationships match the requirement (e.g., shift swaps require coordinators)

JSON OUTPUT:
{
  "tables": {
    "Hospital": {
      "*HospitalID": "int",
      "Name": "varchar(100)",
      "State": "varchar(50)",
      "Address": "varchar(200)"
    },
    "Department": {
      "*DepartmentID": "int",
      "Name": "varchar(50)",
      "HospitalID": "int",
      "CoordinatorID": "int"
    },
    "Person": {
      "*PersonID": "int",
      "FullName": "varchar(100)",
      "CPF": "varchar(14)",
      "RG": "varchar(20)",
      "DOB": "date"
    },
    "Patient": {
      "*PersonID": "int",
      "WristbandID": "varchar(20)"
    },
    "Visitor": {
      "*PersonID": "int",
      "VisitorType": "varchar(50)",
      "CompanionStatus": "varchar(50)",
      "IsolationReason": "text"
    },
    "Employee": {
      "*PersonID": "int",
      "Role": "varchar(50)",
      "ContractType": "varchar(50)"
    },
    "Address": {
      "*AddressID": "int",
      "PersonID": "int",
      "AddressDetails": "text",
      "LastUpdated": "timestamp"
    },
    "IdentificationCard": {
      "*CardID": "int",
      "PersonID": "int",
      "IssueDate": "date",
      "ExpiryDate": "date",
      "CardType": "varchar(50)",
      "ActiveStatus": "boolean"
    },
    "WorkShift": {
      "*ShiftID": "int",
      "EmployeeID": "int",
      "StartDateTime": "datetime",
      "EndDateTime": "datetime"
    },
    "TimeClockRecord": {
      "*RecordID": "int",
      "EmployeeID": "int",
      "ClockIn": "datetime",
      "ClockOut": "datetime"
    },
    "AvailabilityException": {
      "*ExceptionID": "int",
      "EmployeeID": "int",
      "StartDate": "date",
      "EndDate": "date",
      "ExceptionType": "varchar(50)",
      "Notes": "text"
    },
    "ShiftSwap": {
      "*SwapID": "int",
      "Employee1ID": "int",
      "Employee2ID": "int",
      "CoordinatorID": "int",
      "SwapDate": "date",
      "Justification": "text"
    },
    "PatientMovement": {
      "*MovementID": "int",
      "PatientID": "int",
      "DepartmentID": "int",
      "Timestamp": "datetime",
      "Status": "varchar(50)"
    },
    "DepartmentCapacityAlert": {
      "*AlertID": "int",
      "DepartmentID": "int",
      "AlertLevel": "varchar(10)",
      "Timestamp": "datetime"
    }
  },
  "relations": [
    "Hospital:HospitalID 1--* Department:HospitalID",
    "Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID",
    "Person:PersonID 1--1 Patient:PersonID",
    "Person:PersonID 1--1 Visitor:PersonID",
    "Person:PersonID 1--1 Employee:PersonID",
    "Person:PersonID 1--* Address:PersonID",
    "Employee:PersonID 1--* IdentificationCard:PersonID",
    "Employee:PersonID 1--* WorkShift:EmployeeID",
    "Employee:PersonID 1--* TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID",
    "Employee:PersonID 1--* AvailabilityException:EmployeeID",
    "Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID",
    "Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID",
    "Patient:PersonID 1--* PatientMovement:PatientID",
    "Department:DepartmentID 1--* PatientMovement:DepartmentID",
    "Department:DepartmentID 1--* DepartmentCapacityAlert:DepartmentID"
  ],
  "rankAdjustments": "",
  "label": "Integrated Hospital Management System ER Model"
}
